card.title: Groupthink: When Smart Groups Get Dumb
card.teaser: Put eight brilliant people in a room and they can still choose the worst answer. Here's why.
card.coverImagePrompt: A dim wood-paneled conference room where eight silhouetted figures nod in unison around an oval table, a single dissenting chair turned slightly away, cinematic side lighting, moody

lessons.title: The smartest room in Washington
lessons.body: In April 1961, John F. Kennedy sat with a circle of Ivy League advisors — men described by one journalist as 'the best and the brightest.' Together, they approved the Bay of Pigs invasion: 1,400 Cuban exiles landing on a defended beach with no air cover. Within three days the operation was in ruins. Kennedy later asked the question everyone asks: 'How could we have been so stupid?'

visual.imagePrompt: A vintage 1960s White House situation room, men in dark suits leaning over a map of Cuba, cigarette smoke curling under a green banker's lamp

lessons.title: Irving Janis names the disease
lessons.body: Psychologist Irving Janis read the Bay of Pigs transcripts in the 1970s and noticed something strange: the advisors had private doubts, but almost none of them voiced them. He coined the term 'groupthink' for a mode of thinking where the desire for harmony overrides the realistic appraisal of alternatives. The smarter and more cohesive the group, he argued, the more vulnerable they often are.

keyTerms.definition: A pattern where group cohesion suppresses dissent and leads to poor decisions
The degree to which members of a group feel bonded and want to stay united

visual.imagePrompt: A 1970s hardcover psychology book open on a desk, yellow highlighter strokes over the word 'groupthink'

lessons.title: The symptoms
lessons.body: Janis identified eight warning signs. A few stand out: an illusion of invulnerability ('we can't fail'), self-appointed 'mindguards' who shield the leader from inconvenient facts, and direct pressure on doubters ('don't rock the boat'). Individually each symptom is mild. Stacked together, they form a filter that lets only agreeable information reach the decision.

visual.imagePrompt: A chalkboard with eight handwritten symptoms in chalk, three underlined in red

lessons.title: Challenger, 1986
lessons.body: Twenty-five years after the Bay of Pigs, NASA engineers at Morton Thiokol warned that the shuttle's O-rings could fail in cold weather. Under pressure from management, they were asked to 'take off their engineering hat and put on their management hat.' The launch went ahead. Seventy-three seconds after liftoff, Challenger broke apart. The Rogers Commission report reads almost exactly like Janis's checklist.
lessons.funFact: Engineer Roger Boisjoly kept every memo he wrote warning about the O-rings. He testified holding the stack of paper in his hand.

visual.imagePrompt: A 1986 Florida launch pad at dawn, frost visible on the shuttle's side boosters, steam rising into a cold blue sky

lessons.title: How to break the spell
lessons.body: Janis's fix wasn't to add more experts — it was to add friction. Assign a designated devil's advocate. Have the leader speak last so subordinates don't echo them. Invite outside critics into the room. The goal isn't disagreement for its own sake; it's to protect the group from the quiet, polite, catastrophic 'yes.'

quiz.question: Who coined the term 'groupthink'?
quiz.explanation: Irving Janis introduced the concept in the 1970s after analyzing U.S. foreign policy disasters, including the Bay of Pigs.

quiz.question: Which is NOT one of Janis's classic symptoms of groupthink?
quiz.explanation: Rewarding dissent would actually counteract groupthink — it's the opposite of what happens in affected groups.

quiz.question: What's the most practical remedy Janis recommended?
options: Assign a devil's advocate and have leaders speak last
Remove the leader from the room
quiz.explanation: Structural friction — a formal dissent role and holding back leader opinions — protects groups from premature consensus.

sources.title: Report of the Presidential Commission on the Space Shuttle Challenger Accident
